content:
Regression **error metrics** grew from the same **least squares** tradition as training losses. **Gauss** and **Legendre** (early 1800s) minimized squared residuals — the origin of **MSE** and, by square root, **RMSE** as a natural scale-restored summary.

**Laplace** promoted **absolute** deviations, foreshadowing **MAE** as a robust alternative when outliers matter.

Through the 20th century, **standard deviation** σ = √Var(X) in statistics paralleled **RMSE** as √(mean squared deviation) in the same units as the measured quantity — linking evaluation metrics to **Expectation & Variance**.

Applied statistics and forecasting (econometrics, meteorology, operations research) standardized **RMSE** on held-out series. **MAE** gained favor when heavy-tailed errors and robust reporting became common.

Modern ML reused the same formulas: **MSE/MAE as training losses** (see **Loss Functions**) and **RMSE/MAE on test splits** for model cards and leaderboards — sklearn, Kaggle, and production monitoring all treat these as default regression scores.

timeline.label: Least squares (1800s)
timeline.description: Squared-error minimization establishes MSE; RMSE follows as the root-scale summary in physical units.

timeline.label: Robust statistics
timeline.description: Absolute-error methods and MAE emphasized outlier resistance alongside squared-error norms.

timeline.label: Forecasting benchmarks
timeline.description: RMSE becomes the standard holdout score in time-series and econometric model comparison.

timeline.label: ML evaluation practice
timeline.description: Train/val/test splits and libraries (sklearn metrics) separate training loss from reported RMSE/MAE on unseen data.
